conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and I'm looking to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I live in Birmingham.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Now, does Alex live with you in Birmingham?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he lives at home with me. Although I should mention he's been in care for a little while now, but he comes home to stay with me on the weekends.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. I need to understand Alex's situation a bit better to assess your eligibility correctly.

How long has Alex been in care for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been a couple of months now, maybe around 12 weeks or so.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is helpful to know. 

When Alex comes home on weekends, does he stay for at least 2 nights in a row each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does. He comes home every weekend and spends a good amount of time with me - definitely more than 24 hours a week when you add it all up.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. Just to clarify - when Alex comes home on weekends, does he stay for 2 consecutive nights (for example, Friday and Saturday night, or Saturday and Sunday night)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. He stays over Friday and Saturday nights with me every weekend, so two nights in a row.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have one more question to complete my assessment.

Are you regularly spending money on Alex - for example, on things like food, clothes, pocket money, or other items for his care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I still buy him clothes, treats when he comes home, that sort of thing. I'm definitely still contributing to looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: Even though Alex has been in care for around 12 weeks (which is more than the usual 8-week limit), there's an important exception that applies to your situation. You can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in care if they spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you.

Since Alex comes home every weekend and stays for 2 consecutive nights (Friday and Saturday), and you're regularly spending money on his upkeep (clothes, treats, etc.), you meet the criteria for this exception.

**Important:** If you haven't already informed HMRC that Alex has gone into care, you should contact them to update them on your circumstances. They need to know about his situation and the fact that he's spending weekends at home so they can confirm your continued entitlement.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16, meeting the basic age requirement. Although Alex has been in care for approximately 12 weeks (which is more than the standard 8-week limit after which Child Benefit would normally stop), there is an important exception that applies here. According to the official guidance, you may continue to be entitled to Child Benefit for a child in care if they spend at least 24 hours a week at home. In this case, Alex comes home every weekend and stays for 2 consecutive nights (Friday and Saturday), spending more than 24 hours per week at home. Additionally, you are regularly spending money on Alex by buying him clothes and treats. You live in the UK (Birmingham) and are responsible for Alex's care. Therefore, you meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex under the exception for children in care who spend sufficient time at home.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with one child, Alex (age 8). Although Alex has been in care for approximately 12 weeks, he returns home every weekend for 2 consecutive nights, spending more than 24 hours per week at home. The claimant also regularly contributes financially to Alex's upkeep. Under the exception rules for children in care who spend at least 24 hours a week at home, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Additionally, you are regularly spending money on Alex by buying him clothes and treats. You live in the UK (Birmingham) and are responsible for Alex's care. Therefore, you meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex under the exception for children in care who spend sufficient time at home.

Although Alex has been in care for approximately 12 weeks, he returns home every weekend for 2 consecutive nights, spending more than 24 hours per week at home. The claimant also regularly contributes financially to Alex's upkeep. Under the exception rules for children in care who spend at least 24 hours a week at home, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
